lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 16: Vision and Language — Multi-Modal Foundation Models

unit.name: Multimodal language models: LLaVA and Flamingo architectures
unit.description: Introduces vision-language models as image-conditioned autoregressive language models and compares LLaVA and Flamingo fusion mechanisms.
unit.summary: The lecture shifts from image-classification foundation models to multimodal language models, motivated by the flexibility of next-token prediction for adapting to many tasks [ts=1559s] [ts=1582s]. LLaVA-style models ground an autoregressive language model in image tokens: instead of conditioning only on previous words such as “cats are so,” the model also receives visual tokens and generates words about the image [ts=1663s] [ts=1705s]. LLaVA obtains those tokens from a pre-trained CLIP image encoder, using penultimate-layer patch features because final non-CLS tokens are not directly supervised, while penultimate features retain useful spatial information [ts=1748s] [ts=1801s]. A linear connector maps CLIP representations into the token space an LLM can process [ts=1849s]. Flamingo follows the same broad goal but changes the fusion mechanism: instead of feeding visual features only as input tokens, it injects vision features into every LLM layer through a perceiver sampler and gated cross-attention modules [ts=1886s] [ts=1973s]. Most of Flamingo is frozen; the trained parts are the perceiver sampler and cross-attention layers, allowing the language model to decide where and when to attend to visual features [ts=2002s] [ts=2021s].

key_points.text: Multimodal language models try to extend autoregressive language-model reasoning to image-conditioned tasks.
LLaVA feeds image tokens together with text context into a language model to generate image-grounded text.
LLaVA uses a pre-trained CLIP image encoder to create visual tokens.
Penultimate CLIP patch features are useful because final non-CLS tokens are not directly supervised.
A trainable linear layer converts CLIP features into representations understandable by the LLM.
Flamingo adds perceiver sampling and gated cross-attention into LLM layers while freezing most vision and language weights.

kp_catalog.name: Generative multimodal language models
kp_catalog.description: Decoder-centric multimodal systems that condition large language models on visual inputs through projections, cross-attention, or adapter-like components. Vision-language models condition an autoregressive language model on image tokens so visual tasks can be handled through text generation.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because generative multimodal language models formalizes how decoder-centric multimodal systems that condition large language models on visual inputs through projections, cross-attention, or adapter-like components, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between capstone and gateway and importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: LLaVA CLIP-to-LLM connector architecture
kp_catalog.description: LLaVA uses CLIP visual features, typically penultimate patch tokens, and maps them through a trainable linear connector into an LLM.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because lLaVA CLIP-to-LLM connector architecture clarifies lLaVA uses CLIP visual features, typically penultimate patch tokens, and maps them through a trainable linear connector into an LLM, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Flamingo perceiver sampler and gated cross-attention
kp_catalog.description: Flamingo fuses vision into an LLM by sampling visual tokens and injecting them into every layer through gated cross-attention modules.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because flamingo perceiver sampler and gated cross-attention clarifies flamingo fuses vision into an LLM by sampling visual tokens and injecting them into every layer through gated cross-attention modules, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
